description: The provided image displays a 'Check for updates' badge with no discernible scientific content; therefore, visual analysis is unavailable and the following description relies entirely on the provided paper context. Based on the text extraction summary, this Figure likely presents electron channeling contrast imaging (ECCI) and electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) micrographs of an austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel, comparing uncharged samples with those hydrogen-charged to 101 mass ppm. The micrographs reveal hydrogen-enhanced deformation banding (HEDB) manifested as macroscopic kink bands (10–30 μm thick) in <001>//TA oriented grains and submicron deformation bands (0.5–1 μm) in <111>//TA grains. Additionally, hydrogen-enhanced microbanding (HEMB) appears as localized microbands (150–200 nm thick, 1.5–4 μm long) along geometrically necessary boundaries (GNBs) with increased misorientation angles (θ_GNB^H increased by ~1°). Type 2 regions show refined dislocation cells (220 nm to 192 nm), while Type 3 structures exhibit dense dislocation walls and microbands. These hydrogen-induced microstructural modifications facilitate plastic relaxation and geometrically necessary dislocation storage, contributing to the steel's exceptional hydrogen embrittlement resistance (IHE index = -0.045).
